parts.title: Transcript
parts.annotated_transcript_html:
Speaker 1: You now have some time to look at the questions on your screen. Now answer the questions on your screen. Good afternoon everyone, and welcome to our series of lectures on great works of engineering.

Speaker 1: Today we'll be talking about the inner workings of the plasma television screen, one of the great inventions of our time. Have you ever looked at your television screen and wondered what is happening to produce those images? The technology behind your TV screen is, for me, even more exciting than the shows you're watching.

Speaker 1: Today I will be talking about two main types of screen, the CRT monitor, and most importantly, the plasma TV screen, which is now sadly obsolete. It never replaced the CRT monitor in the same way that the LCD screen did, because despite its clear image and bright colours, consumers were put off by the high cost of the plasma screen, and as such it never achieved more than a 10% market share.

Speaker 1: Like the CRT, the plasma TV industry has seen a sharp decline, with countries like Japan ceasing production in 2010, followed by the USA and Europe a few years later, although China still manufactures a small number for the domestic market. CRT production has also completely halted all over the globe.

Speaker 1: To understand the plasma TV's success and failure, we need to look at the features of this technology compared with the CRT screen. CRT stands for cathode ray tube, and it's the screen which has been in use since the 1930s, and is still used in outdated equipment today.

Speaker 1: By contrast, the idea for the plasma screen, while first described in the 30s, wasn't put into production until the 60s. One of the main issues with CRT monitors that the plasma screen resolved was the size limitation for home use. It wasn't safe to create TVs much bigger than a mere 30 inches before plasma came along and allowed TV manufacturers to significantly expand the screen to great dimensions.

Speaker 1: The reason for this limitation lies in the CRT's vacuum tube, which carries the risk of exploding if not disposed of carefully. This makes recycling problematic. Similarly, plasma TVs contain harmful metals such as mercury in their light-emitting cells that can enter the ground and cause damage to local wildlife. As a result, several countries have strict regulations on the disposal of all types of television set.
